title: Chocolate digestives
subtitle: Wholemeal oat biscuits with a dark chocolate back
excerpt: Homemade digestives made with wholemeal flour and porridge oats, baked until crisp, then dipped chocolate-side down in melted dark chocolate and left to set. Makes about twenty-four.
sourceNotes: The digestive biscuit was first produced commercially in Britain in 1892, formulated as a plain wholemeal-and-oat biscuit. The name refers to the bicarbonate of soda used as a leavening agent, then believed to aid digestion. As a widely reproduced domestic recipe it entered the public-domain household-baking literature by the early twentieth century. The chocolate-coated version dates to Mcvitie's 1925 introduction; the domestic recipe simply applies the same dipping technique to the plain base. USDA public-domain publications on grain-based baking document the oat-and-wholemeal biscuit construction.

recipe.batchNotes: Double and bake on two trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for 2 weeks once the chocolate is fully set.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; the biscuits should be deep brown at the edge and set firm across the top before they come out

content.text: A digestive biscuit is a wholemeal-oat shortbread. The proportion of oats to flour is what gives it the coarse, slightly sandy texture. The biscuits need to be fully baked and completely cold before dipping; a warm biscuit will soften the chocolate and prevent it setting clean.

Combine the {{wholemeal-flour}}, {{porridge-oats}}, {{demerara-sugar}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl. Rub in the {{unsalted-butter}} until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs; a few larger flakes of butter are fine. Add the {{whole-milk}} and mix until a stiff dough comes together. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Preheat the oven to 175°C fan. Roll the dough on a lightly floured surface to 4 mm thick; the oats make it harder to roll thinner without cracking. Cut rounds with a 6 cm cutter and space 3 cm apart on lined trays. Prick each round three times with a fork. Bake for 12–14 minutes until the edges are deep brown and the surface feels set when lightly pressed. The centre may still give slightly; it will firm on the tray. Leave to cool completely — at least 30 minutes.

Melt the {{dark-chocolate}} in a heatproof bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water, stirring until smooth. Dip the flat base of each biscuit into the chocolate, coating it evenly, then place chocolate-side up on a sheet of baking paper. Leave at room temperature for 30 minutes until the chocolate is set and matt.
